name: Mus'ab ibn Umair
epithet: The Man Who Gave It All
title: Mus'ab ibn Umair (RA): The Man Who Gave It All
meta_description: The story of Mus'ab ibn Umair (RA), the richest young man in Makkah who gave up everything for Islam, carried the message to Madinah, and died holding the flag at Uhud.
keywords: Mus'ab ibn Umair, story of Musab ibn Umair, first ambassador of Islam, Uhud, companions of the Prophet, sahaba stories, the Firsts
lede: He was the best dressed young man in Makkah. The finest silk, the best perfume, sandals made to measure in Yemen. When he passed through the city, people knew he had been there days later, because his perfume still hung in the air.

This is the story of Mus'ab ibn Umair (may Allah be pleased with him): the richest boy in Makkah, who walked out of his mother's house with nothing, carried Islam to a city that had never seen the Prophet ﷺ, and died with the flag still held against his chest.

sections.h2: The most pampered boy in Makkah
paras: Mus'ab came from Banu Abd al-Dar, one of the smallest and richest clans of Quraysh. He was the only child of his mother, Khunnas bint Malik, who never remarried and built her whole life around him. He inherited a great fortune from his father, and it was said that his wealth alone was close to half the wealth of all Makkah.

His mother spoiled him completely. As a small child he wore gold and silk. As a young man, what Mus'ab wore became the trend across the city, and people would dilute their own perfume just to smell a little like his.

Yet he carried none of it with cruelty. There is no story of him striking a servant or belittling anyone. He was handsome, clever, and well loved: the last young man in Makkah you would expect to turn his back on any of it.

Mus'ab heard things early, because people were always around him. So he heard about a house where the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ was quietly gathering a small group, speaking about one God and the life to come. He was curious, but he did not want his mother to find out.

He went at night, treading softly, and knocked gently on the door of Dar al-Arqam. Inside he found the people no one expected him to sit with: the poor, the enslaved, those without a tribe to protect them. The Prophet ﷺ was speaking about Paradise and the Fire, and he did not stop.

Mus'ab listened, asked a few questions, and accepted Islam that night. He kept it hidden and became a regular student in that house, learning from the Prophet ﷺ and praying in secret near the Kaaba when he could.

sections.h2: Tied up in his own home
paras: One day a man saw Mus'ab praying near the Kaaba and went straight to his mother. She refused to believe it until they brought her son forward and asked him directly. Mus'ab did not lie. He admitted that he had followed the Prophet ﷺ.

His mother could not bring herself to strike the son she adored. Instead she had him confined to a corner of the house and told him he would not leave until he gave up his religion. She cut back his food, and she waited for him to break.

He did not break. In that confinement he recited the Qur'an he had memorised, in a voice the companions loved to hear. He never raised it at his mother. He simply waited, until he learned that some of the Muslims were leaving for Abyssinia. When his guard fell asleep, he slipped away and joined them, with nothing but the clothes on his back.

In Abyssinia he married Hamnah bint Jahsh and began to piece his life back together. When a rumour reached them that Makkah had accepted Islam, he returned, only to find it untrue. The first place he went was to the Prophet ﷺ, and only then to his mother.

She was hurt that he had gone to the Prophet ﷺ first. He answered her gently. She had brought him from her womb into the world, he said, but through the Prophet ﷺ, Allah had brought him from darkness into light. She offered him his wealth and his clothes back if he would only return to his old way. He offered her something better: that she come to Allah and be forgiven.

When she threatened to confine him again, his answer this time was firm. He told her plainly to leave him to his faith, and he would leave her in peace. She told him to leave behind every garment and every coin. He said, take it all, I want nothing, and walked out of the house that should have made him the richest man in Makkah without a single dirham to his name. His mother would die without faith, and that grief stayed with him.

Years later, six young men from Yathrib met the Prophet ﷺ during the Hajj season and accepted Islam. They asked him to send someone back with them to teach their people. The Prophet ﷺ chose Mus'ab: the man who had once owned everything and now owned almost nothing.

In Yathrib he taught quietly, group by group. When a chief named Usayd ibn Hudayr came at him with a spear, Mus'ab stayed calm and simply asked him to sit and listen, and to leave in peace if he did not like what he heard. Usayd listened, and accepted Islam. Then he brought Sa'd ibn Mu'adh, the leader of his people, who came ready to drive Mus'ab out and instead sat and believed. Then came Sa'd ibn Ubadah. The most powerful men in the city had become Muslim in a single afternoon.

Mus'ab stayed for about a year as their teacher and imam, far from the Prophet ﷺ he loved. He did not only make people Muslim. He made them love the Prophet ﷺ so deeply that they could not wait to meet him. When the Prophet ﷺ finally reached Madinah, the people were already waiting in the streets with the Qur'an on their lips.

By now the young man whose perfume once filled a room owned a single coarse cloak, patched and worn. Ali ibn Abi Talib (may Allah be pleased with him) remembered the Prophet ﷺ looking at Mus'ab in that one rough garment and weeping, remembering the silk and gold this same man had worn in Makkah. He had turned his heart from this world so completely that there was nothing left in it to pull him back.

Because of all he had given and all he had become, Mus'ab was chosen to carry the flag of the Muslims at Badr, and again at Uhud. At Badr he saw one of his own brothers among the captives of Quraysh, and the ties that bound him now were ties of faith, not of blood. The flag bearer held the morale of the whole army; while the banner stood, the army knew it was still in the fight. At Uhud, when the line broke and the enemy pushed toward the Prophet ﷺ, Mus'ab carried the flag away from him on purpose, raising it high and calling out so the attack would follow him instead.

A man struck off his right hand, and Mus'ab took the flag in his left. His left hand was cut too, and he held the flag against his chest with what remained of his arms. He kept reciting as he stood, until a spear ran him through and killed him. He resembled the Prophet ﷺ so closely that some thought it was the Prophet ﷺ himself who had fallen.
The last words on Mus'ab's lips were a reminder to the believers not to lose heart, even if their Prophet ﷺ were taken from them:

ayah.text: Muhammad is only a messenger before whom many messengers have been and gone. If he died or was killed, would you revert to your old ways? If anyone did so, he would not harm God in the least. God will reward the grateful.

sections.h2: A cloth too short to cover him
paras: He was found with more than seventy wounds on his body. When the companions came to shroud him, they found only one short cloth. When they covered his head, his feet showed, and when they covered his feet, his head showed. The Prophet ﷺ told them to cover his head and lay grass over his legs. This was the man who had once dressed in the finest silk in Makkah.
The Prophet ﷺ stood over him and wept, and bore witness that he was among the martyrs of Allah. Over the fallen of Uhud he recited a verse about the believers who kept their word:

Long after, the companions could not speak of goodness without remembering him. Khabbab, who had been tortured in Makkah, said that some of them lived to taste the rewards of this world, but Mus'ab died before any of it came, with only the reward of the next life waiting for him. Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf, who became wealthy, wept over his food and said that Mus'ab was better than him, and had nothing but one cloth to be buried in.

He left behind only a baby daughter and not a single saying recorded in his name. But he left something larger. Think of the whole city he taught, and everyone after them who prayed and believed because of it. On the Day of Judgement, the scholars have said, a man could meet Allah with all of that weighed in his favour. Mus'ab gave up the finest life in Makkah, and what he carried away was beyond anything he set down.

ayah.text: There are men among the believers who honoured their pledge to God: some of them have fulfilled it by death, and some are still waiting. They have not changed in the least.

lessons_intro: His story is the story of a man who gave up everything the world could offer and asked for nothing back. That is exactly why the companions never forgot him.

lessons.text: Mus'ab had the most to lose, and he lost it willingly. Real conviction will cost you something, and he paid it in full, without bitterness.
He met a raised spear with calm and a fair offer, and won the man over. He brought a whole city to Islam not by force but by how he carried himself.

lessons.bold: You are someone's first picture of faith.
lessons.text: People in Madinah loved the Prophet ﷺ before they met him, because of how one man lived. Those around us often meet the Prophet ﷺ through us first.
He never saw the ease that later believers enjoyed, and he would not have traded what waited for him for any of it.

faq.a: An early companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ from Makkah, often called the first ambassador of Islam. He was sent to teach the people of Madinah, and later died as a martyr carrying the flag at the Battle of Uhud.

faq.q: Why is Mus'ab ibn Umair so well remembered?
faq.a: He was the richest and best dressed young man in Makkah, yet he gave up all of it for Islam. He carried the message to Madinah, and he died holding the Muslim flag at Uhud with more than seventy wounds on his body.

faq.q: What happened to Mus'ab at the Battle of Uhud?
faq.a: He carried the flag of the Muslims. When the enemy pushed toward the Prophet ﷺ, he drew their attention to himself. His right hand was cut, then his left, and he held the flag with his arms until he was killed.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Mus'ab ibn Umair?
faq.a: That sincere faith may cost us comfort and status, that good character can change hearts where force cannot, and that what Allah preserves for us is greater than anything we give up.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us are never asked to give up a fortune. We are asked for smaller things: an honest answer when a lie would be easier, a habit we know we should leave, a comfort we hold a little too tightly. Mus'ab's life makes those choices feel real. He shows that faith is measured not by what we say we believe, but by what we are willing to let go of for it.

He died young, with almost nothing, and the companions who lived to see wealth and ease still wept when they remembered him. Carry one thing from him into today: hold this world a little more loosely, treat people a little more gently, and let what you believe actually cost you something. That is when a story like his has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Mus'ab ibn Umair (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Qur'an translations are from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 3:144 and 33:23). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported. Auto-caption spellings of names have been corrected to standard seerah spelling.

The finest silk. The best perfume. Sandals made to measure in Yemen.

Then he gave all of it away.

His wealth alone was close to half the wealth of Makkah.

People diluted their perfume just to smell a little like Mus'ab ibn Umair.

He heard about a quiet house where the Prophet ﷺ was teaching.

He went at night, knocked softly, and accepted Islam that evening, sitting among the poor and the enslaved.

His mother could not strike the son she adored.

So she tied him up in their home and waited for him to break.

He did not break.

She offered him his wealth and his clothes back.

He said: take it all, I want nothing.

And walked out without a single coin to his name.

The Prophet ﷺ sent him to teach a whole city.

A man came at him with a spear. Mus'ab calmly asked him to sit and listen.

The man accepted Islam. Soon the whole city followed.

At Uhud he carried the flag.

His right hand was cut. He took it in his left.

His left was cut. He held it with his arms, until a spear took his life.

**What Allah keeps is greater.**

They buried him in one cloth too short to cover him. He never saw ease in this world, and would not have traded what waited for him.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
Mus'ab ibn Umair (may Allah be pleased with him) was the richest, best dressed young man in Makkah. People copied his clothes and diluted their perfume to smell like him.

Then he met the Prophet ﷺ in a quiet house, and accepted Islam. His mother confined him to break his faith. When that failed, she offered him all his wealth back if he would return to his old way. He told her to take everything, and walked out with nothing.

He carried Islam to Madinah, then carried the flag at Uhud, where he held it with his arms after both his hands were cut, until he was killed. They buried him in a single cloth too short to cover his whole body.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/musab-ibn-umair

email.subject: The richest boy in Makkah who walked out with nothing
email.alt_subject: Mus'ab ibn Umair: the man who gave it all
email.preview: He had the finest silk and the best perfume in the city. Then he gave all of it away.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Mus'ab ibn Umair (may Allah be pleased with him) was the best dressed young man in Makkah. He wore the finest silk and the best perfume, and had sandals made to measure in Yemen. People copied the way he dressed, and diluted their perfume just to smell a little like him. His wealth alone was said to be close to half the wealth of the whole city.

Then he heard about a quiet house where the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ was teaching a small group about one God and the life to come. He went at night, knocked softly on the door, and accepted Islam that evening, sitting among the poor and the enslaved that no one expected him to sit with.

When his mother found out, she could not bring herself to strike the son she adored. So she confined him to a corner of the house and waited for him to give up his faith. He did not. He recited the Qur'an quietly and waited, until he could slip away and migrate with the other Muslims.

Later she offered him everything back, all his wealth and his fine clothes, if he would only return to his old way. He told her to take it all, that he wanted nothing, and walked out of that house without a single coin to his name.

The Prophet ﷺ trusted him with one task: to carry Islam to the city of Madinah. He taught it so beautifully that the whole city was waiting in the streets for the Prophet ﷺ before he even arrived.

At the Battle of Uhud, Mus'ab carried the flag. When the enemy pushed toward the Prophet ﷺ, he drew them to himself instead. His right hand was cut, so he held the flag in his left. His left was cut, so he held it with his arms, until a spear took his life.

They buried him in a single cloth too short to cover his whole body: the man who once owned the finest clothes in Makkah. He never saw the ease that later believers enjoyed, and he would not have traded what waited for him for any of it.

May Allah be pleased with him, and gather us in the company of those who gave everything for what they believed.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Mus'ab ibn Umair (may Allah be pleased with him) was the best dressed young man in Makkah. He wore the finest silk and the best perfume, and had sandals made to measure in Yemen. People copied the way he dressed, and his wealth alone was close to half the wealth of the whole city. His mother adored him and spoiled him completely.

Then he heard about a quiet house where the Prophet ﷺ was teaching. He went at night, knocked softly, and accepted Islam that evening, sitting among the poor and the enslaved that no one expected him to sit with. He kept his faith hidden, until a man saw him praying and told his mother.

She could not bring herself to strike the son she loved, so she tied him up in their home and waited for him to break. He did not break. When she later offered him all his wealth and clothes back, he told her to take everything, that he wanted nothing, and walked out without a single coin to his name.

The Prophet ﷺ chose him to carry Islam to the city of Madinah. When a chief came at him with a spear, Mus'ab calmly asked him to sit and listen, and the man accepted Islam. He taught the city so beautifully that the people were waiting in the streets, in love with the Prophet ﷺ, before they had even met him.

At the Battle of Uhud he carried the flag. When the enemy pushed toward the Prophet ﷺ, he drew them to himself. His right hand was cut, so he held the flag in his left. His left was cut, so he held it with his arms, until a spear took his life. He was found with more than seventy wounds.

They buried him in a single cloth too short to cover his whole body. When they covered his head, his feet showed, and when they covered his feet, his head showed. The Prophet ﷺ wept over him, and bore witness that he was among the martyrs of Allah.
storybook.pullquote: Take it all. I want nothing.
storybook.reflection: Mus'ab had more of this world than almost anyone, and he gave every bit of it away for something he could not yet see. His life is a quiet reminder that sincere faith may cost us comfort and status, and that what Allah keeps for us is far greater than anything we leave behind.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
